title: RFC: Fix compilation issues in QEMU machine files (Windows platform)
body:
There are QEMU machine parts related to Windows platform, which were "fast patched" during 5.0 version development with disabling compilation for a part of them and making them unattended. These files accumulated some amount of code rot.  This PR addresses only the code rot issues w/o enabling the actual QEMU Windows support. It does this in a way that also prevents any new warnings in VSCode.

There is expectation that at some point the whole Windows specific parts could be dropped, but I believe that fixing code rot before would not make any harm (especially with a limited scope and changes being compact). The only ugly part is that shared memory backend for virtiofs has to be handled, because this is not supported on Windows. This could count towards added complexity, but I hope this could be acceptable.

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body: I will close this if it will be identified as 100% no go or there is a definitive roadmap to clear these parts out soon.

There is at least one feature missing with the QEMU Windows in the current state - filesystem mounts. So, it would require work to implement and mark it "fully supported" and this effort would not be justified (as this will be actual maintenance burden). I submitted this PR just because I had this changes ready since 5.0 days. Triggering an outcome of a code cleanup will be a good outcome as well, I believe :)
I'm OK with code cleanup.  I too am not onboard with QEMU on Windows.  Lets do the code cleanup and leave as is ... perhaps if we get devs/maintainers who are interested, we can consider it again ?

@arixmkii I have checked out your PR. However, I am still getting some warnings on VS Code when opening the go files in the qemu package on Windows. Can you please share your VS Code settings to remove the warnings?

All errors are marked as `[freebsd, 386]` and I have no good idea how to fix it except of adding `&& (amd64 || arm64)` to every file affected, but it could be a different PR for sure.

> @l0rd I'm running very basic setup of VSCode with Go development recommended extension and no config changes.

Thank you @arixmkii for sharing. I specify the following VS Code settings to get proper code assistance and no warnings:
```json
  "go.buildTags": "remote exclude_graphdriver_btrfs btrfs_noversion exclude_graphdriver_devicemapper containers_image_openpgp",
  "go.useLanguageServer": true,
  "gopls": {
    "ui.semanticTokens": true,
    "build.directoryFilters": ["-", "+cmd/podman"]
  },
```

The changes look good to me and all checks are green
/lgtm
